leadershipThought: Think before you speak. Think before you joke. Think before you act. Ask yourself: if this shows up in front of a client, my family, or leadership — does it represent me well? If the answer is no — don't do it.
exxonMoments:
Good communication between all crews, continuous HS monitoring, and the job (Operational Discipline)
Good communication between all crews, continuous HS monitoring, and the job was

Roustabout crew member is fairly new (Operational Discipline)
Roustabout crew member is fairly new. Wasn't hitting a hammer union in the right

Incomplete OWL Submission — Blank Form (Operational Discipline)
A blank form submitted late in the day suggests end-of-shift fatigue is eroding the debrief habit — completing the debrief on-site before leaving is more reliable than submitting it after.

Incomplete OWL Submission — Blank Form (Operational Discipline)
Blank forms submitted late in the afternoon/evening suggest end-of-day fatigue is a factor in debrief quality — building the debrief into the job closeout on-site is more reliable than completing it later.

Incomplete OWL Submission — Blank Form (Two Technicians, Same Location) (Operational Discipline)
Two blank submissions from the same job at the same time is a crew-level debrief gap — the post-job review should be a shared step led by the senior person on site, not an individual task each person submits alone.

fieldNews.headline: Ray Solis finished at the University and rolled straight to a rig down at H&P 628 the same evening — no gap, no waiting, just moved.
Stefanie Kirschke worked through a cement shut-in delay at Ovintiv Tom Ranch — posted updates all day, confirmed the schedule, and had a clean 7 AM return locked in before she left.
James Stephens put Diamondback Mabee 13-24 1-1H to bed and posted up in Breakroom before heading out. Short message, job done.
Michael Hutt got SM First Tee #121 well opened and back in play — posted the update so dispatch and the team know the location is active.
Robert Wright wrapped the SDU Tract job clean and headed back. Job complete, crew moving — that's all you need to post.
SM Energy Gipple 154H — 2,000ppm H2S. Dennis Easley flagged it Tuesday afternoon. This location is not a walk-up — confirm your monitoring plan before you leave the yard.
Cosmo phase 2 — H2S 3,000ppm and CO 3% on the 9 AM pull. Louie Zarate confirmed both readings. Supplied air is not optional at this location — it is the plan.
Nobles 3202D — crane was not on site. Edward Carrillo made the call: crew stood down, job rescheduled. Confirm your equipment is on location before you dispatch.

todaysChallenge: On your next job, identify the line of fire before anything moves.
todaysChallengeSubtext: Where is the pressure release path? Where is the swing radius? Where should no one stand? Name it before work starts.
